summary: Machine policy for the Diagnostic Data Viewer experience in Settings. Microsoft documents the same device policy control, registry key path, default, and `0/1` meanings that the app uses.

setting.name: Diagnostic Data Viewer
setting.casual_explanation: Windows includes a viewer that can show diagnostic data categories on the device. This policy decides whether that viewer is available.
setting.why_people_change_it: Some environments disable the viewer to reduce diagnostic-data tooling on managed devices or to simplify the surface available to users.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling the viewer reduces access to a diagnostic-data inspection tool, but it also removes a built-in transparency feature that can help users or admins review diagnostic data locally.
setting.professional_notes: DataCollection.admx maps this machine policy to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection\DisableDiagnosticDataViewer with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the Diagnostic Data Viewer policy.

allowed_values.label: Diagnostic Data Viewer allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and the viewer remains available.

allowed_values.label: Diagnostic Data Viewer disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The viewer is blocked by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableDiagnosticDataViewer = 1, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 1809 and newer supported systems

states.rationale: The machine policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: This describes policy state only and does not imply that every edition exposes the same UI surface.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who want built-in visibility into diagnostic data
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the built-in viewer to stay available if Windows supports it.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a harder privacy or management baseline that removes this tool.
tradeoffs: Users can inspect supported diagnostic-data views locally.
No explicit restriction is enforced for the viewer.

recommended_profiles.label: Managed privacy
avoid_for: Users or admins who want the local viewer for transparency and troubleshooting
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to reduce built-in diagnostic-data tooling on the device.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to inspect diagnostic-data categories through the Windows UI.
tradeoffs: The Diagnostic Data Viewer is blocked.
Users lose a built-in way to review supported diagnostic-data output.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: DisableDiagnosticDataViewer
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that controls access to the Diagnostic Data Viewer experience.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft DataCollection.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows DisableDiagnosticDataViewer with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under the DataCollection policy key.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableDiagnosticDataViewer = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

evidence.title: nohuto win-config mirror - DisableDiagnosticDataViewer policy
evidence.summary: DataCollection.admx shows DisableDiagnosticDataViewer under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection. The mirrored ExplainText says enabling the policy prevents the Diagnostic Data Viewer from being enabled or showing Microsoft diagnostic data, while disabling or not configuring it leaves the viewer enabled. EnabledValue=1 and DisabledValue=0 are explicit.

decision.why: Microsoft documents DisableDiagnosticDataViewer directly in the System Policy CSP, including the device scope, default value, allowed values, and the group policy mapping to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection. The app writes that exact machine policy surface with the documented disable value 1.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: DisableDiagnosticDataViewer ... Default Value 0 ... Allowed values: 0 (Default) Not disabled. 1 Disabled. Group policy mapping ... Name DisableDiagnosticDataViewer ... Registry Key Name Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection
validation_proof.notes: The System Policy CSP page explicitly names the policy control, gives the default and allowed values, and maps it to the same DataCollection machine policy key the app writes. Added nohuto mirror corroboration via nohuto-datacollection-viewer-admx.
